refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency at the 95th percentile (p95) ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, response time to hazards, and control precision. The p95 metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command input to sensor feedback display) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using standardized test scenarios. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition. Validate against 150 ms threshold with safety margin analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands over the network link with bounded latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall receive and process remote commands with minimal latency.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The vehicle shall acquire and transmit telemetry feedback with minimal latency.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall receive and display telemetry feedback to the operator with minimal latency.
FR-NGHE-008.6: The teleoperation system shall implement latency monitoring and alerting to detect degradation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement end-to-end latency budget allocation across command path (operator input → network → vehicle processing → feedback acquisition) and feedback path (sensor acquisition → network → operator display), with per-stage latency targets and real-time monitoring.

DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (5G/LTE with QoS prioritization) and optimize software stack (interrupt-driven I/O, lock-free queues, hardware timestamping) to minimize processing delays.
DP-C: Implement predictive control algorithms and local autonomy fallback to reduce operator reaction time dependency and tolerate transient latency spikes.
DP-D: Establish latency measurement framework with instrumentation at command input, network ingress/egress, vehicle processing, sensor acquisition, and operator display stages.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-D: Implement a hierarchical latency budget allocation strategy (DP-A) paired with comprehensive latency instrumentation and monitoring (DP-D). This approach decouples the logical latency requirement from specific technology choices, enabling allocation across hardware, network, and software layers. Instrumentation provides visibility for verification and operational monitoring.

potential_sources: DP-A couples to network infrastructure capability (must support bounded latency); requires DP-B technology decisions downstream.
DP-D couples to telemetry and tasking interface design (must include timestamp metadata); requires coordination with Vehicle and Teleoperation module interfaces.
Latency budget allocation couples to vehicle processing architecture and network link characteristics; not fully decoupled but manageable via interface contracts.
coupling_check.notes: Selected DP avoids premature technology lock-in (e.g., specific network type) while establishing measurable allocation framework. Coupling is acceptable at this level; will be resolved in sub-level decomposition.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network link provides bounded latency characteristics (jitter, packet loss) within acceptable ranges; agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle processing stack can be optimized to meet allocated latency budget (typically 30–50 ms for command processing + feedback acquisition).
ASSUMPTION: Operator display and input hardware introduce <20 ms latency; operator cognitive response time is not part of this requirement.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed parameters, p95 target may not be achievable without fallback autonomy or operator notification.
RISK: Latency budget allocation may be infeasible if any single stage (e.g., vehicle sensor acquisition) inherently exceeds allocated time.
RISK: Instrumentation overhead (timestamps, logging) could introduce additional latency if not carefully implemented.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly define latency budget allocation strategy: (1) specify total budget breakdown (e.g., 40 ms network, 50 ms vehicle processing, 30 ms operator display, 30 ms margin); (2) define fallback behavior if any stage exceeds budget; (3) clarify whether p95 applies to individual stages or end-to-end only. Ensure DP-D instrumentation design includes low-overhead timestamp mechanisms (hardware clocks, minimal serialization).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency requirement and serves as the primary integration point for latency budget allocation, monitoring, and verification. It coordinates command transmission, feedback reception, and latency instrumentation across the operator interface and network link. The module is responsible for latency visibility and alerting.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation transmits operator commands (remote control inputs) to Vehicle via Tasking interface. Latency budget: ~40 ms for network transmission + Vehicle ingress.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives vehicle state, sensor data, and feedback from Vehicle via Telemetry interface. Latency budget: ~50 ms for Vehicle acquisition + network transmission + Teleoperation ingress.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation interfaces with Network Link for command and telemetry transmission. Network Link is responsible for bounded latency delivery; Teleoperation monitors and reports latency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives safety alerts from Safety Services (e.g., latency degradation warnings, emergency stop signals). Latency budget: <10 ms for alert propagation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency requirement. It directly controls operator interface latency, command transmission timing, feedback reception, and display latency. Latency instrumentation and monitoring are core Teleoperation responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only network link characteristics (owned by Network Link) and vehicle processing (owned by Vehicle) are external dependencies.

Vehicle owns command processing and sensor acquisition latency (sub-components of end-to-end latency), but does not own operator interface or network transmission latency. Assigning the full requirement to Vehicle would diffuse responsibility for network and operator-side latency, making verification and ownership unclear.

Safety Services may monitor latency as a safety indicator and trigger alerts, but does not own the latency requirement itself. Assigning ownership to Safety Services would create diffusion: latency is a performance/operational requirement, not a safety function. Safety Services should react to latency violations, not own the requirement.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too abstract to own a specific latency requirement. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all sub-modules without clear ownership or accountability. Latency must be owned by a concrete module (Teleoperation) with direct control over interfaces.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it directly controls operator input latency and display latency; (2) it manages command transmission via Tasking interface and feedback reception via Telemetry interface; (3) it interfaces with Network Link and can monitor/report network latency; (4) it can implement latency instrumentation and alerting; (5) it has clear accountability for end-to-end latency performance. Vehicle and Safety Services are dependencies, not owners.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle processing latency exceeds allocated budget, Teleoperation cannot directly control it. Mitigation: establish latency SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle; implement fallback autonomy or operator notification.

RISK: If Network Link introduces unpredictable latency spikes, Teleoperation may not achieve p95 target. Mitigation: define agreed network conditions; implement latency monitoring and alerting; coordinate with network operator.

RISK: Latency instrumentation overhead could introduce additional latency if not carefully implemented. Mitigation: use hardware timestamps and lock-free data structures; validate instrumentation impact separately.

RISK: Operator display hardware (monitor, input device) latency is outside Teleoperation module scope but contributes to end-to-end latency. Mitigation: establish operator interface latency budget; document assumptions about operator hardware.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify interface contracts: (1) Tasking interface must include command timestamp and latency budget metadata; (2) Telemetry interface must include sensor acquisition timestamp and vehicle processing latency; (3) Network Link interface must provide latency metrics (one-way delay, jitter); (4) SafetyAlert interface must support latency degradation alerts. Define latency SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Vehicle commits to <50 ms p95 for command processing + feedback acquisition. Establish monitoring and escalation procedures for latency violations.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (latency budget allocation + instrumentation) directly addresses FR (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). DP is solution-neutral and enables verification through measurable latency targets at each stage. DP does not over-specify technology (e.g., network type) and allows flexibility in implementation. Coupling is acceptable and will be resolved in sub-level decomposition.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency but depends on Vehicle processing latency and Network Link performance. Mitigation: establish clear SLAs and interface contracts; implement monitoring and alerting.

Latency instrumentation (DP-D) requires coordination across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link modules. Mitigation: define instrumentation interface standards (timestamp format, metadata); centralize latency aggregation in Teleoperation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the appropriate owner with high containment score (95). External dependencies (Vehicle, Network Link) are manageable via interface contracts and monitoring. No significant diffusion risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) measure end-to-end latency using instrumentation at command input, network ingress/egress, vehicle processing, sensor acquisition, and operator display; (2) collect p95 statistics over representative network conditions; (3) validate against 150 ms threshold. Acceptance criteria are clear and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency requirement and is responsible for latency budget allocation, monitoring, and verification. Sub-requirements (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.6) are allocated to specific stages (operator input, network transmission, vehicle processing, feedback acquisition, operator display, monitoring). Ownership boundaries are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation in DP-A: specify target latency for each stage (operator input, network transmission, vehicle processing, feedback acquisition, operator display) and margin for contingency.
Define latency SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Vehicle commits to <50 ms p95 for command processing + feedback acquisition.
Establish interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, and SafetyAlert: include timestamp metadata, latency metrics, and alert signals.
Document assumptions about operator hardware latency and network conditions; establish acceptance criteria for network conditions.
Plan latency instrumentation design: specify timestamp mechanisms (hardware clocks, minimal serialization), data collection, aggregation, and reporting.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies six sub-requirements (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.6) covering command acquisition, transmission, vehicle processing, feedback acquisition, display, and monitoring. Each sub-requirement must be decomposed to concrete DPs and IMs. The selected DP (latency budget allocation + instrumentation) is sound but requires detailed allocation across stages. Teleoperation module assignment is appropriate and passes all gates. Proceed to next decomposition level for sub-requirements.
decision.justification_detail: The requirement is complex and multi-stage (operator input → network → vehicle → feedback → operator display). Latency budget allocation requires explicit targets for each stage. Instrumentation design requires detailed specification of timestamp mechanisms and data collection. Sub-requirements must be individually analyzed to ensure no latency source is overlooked. Decomposition will enable verification planning and detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency (target: <10 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands over the network link with bounded latency (target: <40 ms p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall receive and process remote commands with minimal latency (target: <30 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The vehicle shall acquire and transmit telemetry feedback with minimal latency (target: <20 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall receive and display telemetry feedback to the operator with minimal latency (target: <30 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.6: The teleoperation system shall implement latency monitoring and alerting to detect degradation (alert threshold: >150 ms p95 or >120 ms p50).

assumptions: Network link provides bounded latency characteristics (jitter, packet loss) within agreed parameters; agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable.
Vehicle processing stack can be optimized to meet allocated latency budget (~50 ms for command processing + feedback acquisition).
Operator display and input hardware introduce <20 ms latency; operator cognitive response time is not part of this requirement.
Latency is measured end-to-end from operator command input to feedback display; does not include operator reaction time.
p95 metric is applied to end-to-end latency; individual stages may have different statistical distributions.
Teleoperation module has authority to implement latency instrumentation and monitoring across interfaces.
Safety Services will react to latency violations but does not own the latency requirement.
open_risks: RISK: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, making p95 target unachievable. Mitigation: define fallback behavior (operator notification, autonomy activation, operation suspension).

RISK: Vehicle processing latency may exceed allocated budget due to hardware constraints or software inefficiency. Mitigation: establish latency SLA; implement fallback autonomy or operator notification.
RISK: Latency instrumentation overhead could introduce additional latency. Mitigation: use hardware timestamps and lock-free data structures; validate instrumentation impact separately.
RISK: Operator display hardware latency is outside module scope but contributes to end-to-end latency. Mitigation: establish operator interface latency budget; document assumptions.
RISK: Latency budget allocation may be infeasible if any single stage inherently exceeds allocated time. Mitigation: conduct feasibility analysis during detailed design; adjust budget if necessary.
evidence_needed: Network latency characterization: measure one-way delay, jitter, packet loss under agreed network conditions.
Vehicle processing latency baseline: measure command processing and feedback acquisition latency on target hardware.
Operator interface latency baseline: measure input device and display latency on target operator station.
Latency instrumentation design: specify timestamp mechanisms, data collection, aggregation, and reporting.
Latency budget allocation validation: confirm that allocated targets for each stage are achievable and sum to <150 ms p95.
Latency monitoring and alerting design: specify alert thresholds, escalation procedures, and operator notifications.
Safety analysis: confirm that latency degradation does not create unsafe conditions; define fallback behaviors.
